topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The late afternoon light, thin and watery, slanted across my desk, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air. I was tracing the faded ink of a particularly sad passage in my journal, the words mirroring the dull ache settling in my chest. When I finally looked up, a strange tension tightened in my throat. My roommate, Alex, was sitting in the armchair across the room, ostensibly studying, but their gaze was fixed entirely on the open book resting on my desk. A slow, almost imperceptible smile touched their lips as they turned a page, a look that felt heavy with unspoken knowledge. My breath hitched. I quickly tried to cover the journal, my fingers trembling slightly as I slid it under a stack of textbooks, the silence in the room suddenly vast and suffocating.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked beneath my pillow: a page from my private diary, the handwriting undeniably mine, but annotated in the margins with Alex’s looping script. A cold wave washed over me, making my skin prickle. I stood frozen by the door, the sound of Alex humming softly from the kitchen grating on my nerves. Every muscle in my body felt heavy, weighted down by an invisible cloak. I walked over, my footsteps sounding unnaturally loud on the hardwood floor. When I presented the journal, my voice came out thin and shaky. Alex looked up, their expression unreadable, a flicker of something sharp crossing their eyes before they smoothly offered an explanation that didn't quite settle the unsettling feeling churning in my stomach.

[story 3]

The air in our shared room felt thick, stagnant, mirroring the fog that had settled inside my head all week. I was trying to focus on the dense poetry in my notebook, but my attention kept drifting to Alex. They were perched on the edge of their bed, ostensibly scrolling through their phone, yet their eyes kept flicking towards my side of the room, towards the familiar spiral-bound book. When I finally noticed the discrepancy—a specific entry I’d only written for myself was now underlined in a different colored pen—a profound sense of exposure washed over me. I didn't confront them immediately. Instead, I retreated to the bathroom, gripping the cool porcelain sink until my knuckles were white, trying to regulate the rapid, shallow rhythm of my breathing. The quiet observation of their presence felt like a spotlight, intensely unwelcome.
